_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against AAD 2024 Guidelines of Care for the Management of Acne Vulgaris (Reynolds JAAD 2024), AAD 2016 guideline (Zaenglein JAAD 2016), Williams Lancet 2012 seminar, Bhate-Williams BJD 2013 epidemiology, Dreno JEADV 2018 microbiome review, Vallerand BJD 2018 isotretinoin systematic review, and the SAFA RCT (Santer BMJ 2023). Drug doses and screening recommendations cross-checked against current iPLEDGE program guidance and UpToDate Lexicomp.

h1Title: Acne: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acne vulgaris is the most common skin disease in humans, driven by four overlapping problems inside the pilosebaceous follicle: excess sebum production under androgen control, hyperkeratinization of the follicular lining, overgrowth of Cutibacterium acnes biofilms, and the inflammatory cascade that follows. It affects roughly 85% of people aged 12 to 24 and persists into adulthood for about 12% of women and 3% of men, with the Global Burden of Disease study ranking it the eighth most prevalent disease worldwide and the leading skin disease by years lived with disability. The visible lesions range from non-inflammatory comedones to inflammatory papules, pustules, and deep painful nodules that can scar permanently if untreated. Modern therapy clears or near-clears the skin in the large majority of patients within 12 to 24 weeks, using topical retinoids, benzoyl peroxide, oral antibiotics combined with topicals, hormonal therapy in women, and oral isotretinoin for severe disease.

keyStats.prevalence: ~85% of adolescents aged 12-24; 12% of women and 3% of men over 25 (Bhate & Williams BJD 2013)
keyStats.demographics: Earlier onset in girls (mean 11) than boys (mean 12); severe disease more common in adolescent boys, persistent adult disease more common in women
keyStats.avgAge: Peak severity ages 14-19; adult-onset acne after 25 affects ~12% of women
keyStats.globalCases: ~9.4% global prevalence; 8th most prevalent disease worldwide (GBD; Williams Lancet 2012)

definition: Acne vulgaris (ICD-10: L70.0) is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the pilosebaceous unit, the follicular structure that bundles a hair shaft with a sebaceous gland. Four interacting mechanisms produce the clinical disease. First, androgens drive enlargement of the sebaceous gland and excess sebum output beginning at adrenarche, around age 7-8. Second, the lining of the follicle becomes hyperkeratinized, with keratinocytes failing to shed normally and plugging the duct, producing the microcomedo that is the precursor of every acne lesion. Third, the trapped lipid-rich environment promotes overgrowth of Cutibacterium acnes (formerly Propionibacterium acnes), and specific virulent phylotypes, particularly IA1, organize into biofilms that activate innate immunity. Fourth, Toll-like receptor 2 signalling and the NLRP3 inflammasome release interleukin-1 beta, interleukin-8, interleukin-17, and tumour necrosis factor, producing the visible erythema, pus, and pain. Severity is graded using the Investigator's Global Assessment (IGA), the Comprehensive Acne Severity Scale (CASS), and the Global Acne Grading System (GAGS), which guide therapy selection. Acne is managed primarily in dermatology, with substantial overlap with primary care, paediatrics for pre-adolescent cases, and endocrinology when polycystic ovary syndrome or other endocrinopathy is suspected.

typesClassification.description: Non-inflammatory disease dominated by open comedones (blackheads) and closed comedones (whiteheads) across the forehead, nose, and chin. Typical first presentation in early adolescence.
Inflammatory lesions, red papules and pus-tipped pustules, appear alongside comedones, most often on the face but also the upper chest, shoulders, and back. The most common phenotype in clinic.

Deep, painful, dome-shaped nodules and cysts larger than 5 mm, often coalescing into draining sinuses. Almost universally scars without aggressive systemic therapy. Acne conglobata is the most extreme form.

Persistent or late-onset disease in women over 25, typically distributed along the jawline, chin, and lower face with premenstrual flares. Hormonal evaluation indicated when accompanied by hirsutism, irregular periods, or rapid onset.

Rare, abrupt-onset severe ulcerative acne with systemic features such as fever, arthralgia, leukocytosis, and elevated inflammatory markers. Most often in adolescent boys, sometimes triggered by starting isotretinoin without coverage.
Lesions confined to areas of pressure, friction, occlusion, or cosmetic application, such as helmet straps, sports gear, surgical masks (maskne), and pomade-laden scalp margins.

primarySymptoms: Open comedones (blackheads) are small dilated follicular openings filled with oxidized keratin and melanin, most often across the nose, forehead, and chin.
Closed comedones (whiteheads) are small flesh-coloured or whitish bumps roughly 1-2 mm wide that lack an opening to the skin surface and feel rough to the touch.
Inflammatory papules present as red, tender, raised bumps 2-5 mm in diameter without a visible pus head, lasting 4-10 days each.
Pustules are superficial papules topped with a yellow-white pus collection, classically on the forehead and cheeks, that drain or crust within a few days.
Nodules are deep, firm, painful lesions larger than 5 mm that sit under the skin surface, last for weeks, and frequently leave atrophic or hypertrophic scars.
Cysts and draining sinuses are fluctuant deeper collections that can interconnect under the skin, characteristic of severe nodulocystic acne and almost always scarring without systemic therapy.
Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation produces flat brown or grey-brown marks lingering for months after lesions resolve, more prominent and longer lasting in skin of colour.
Post-inflammatory erythema causes flat pink to red marks that follow inflammatory lesions, more visible in lighter skin types and typically fading over 3-6 months.
Scarring takes atrophic (ice-pick, boxcar, rolling), hypertrophic, or keloidal forms that appear after nodules and cysts and persist long after the active disease.
Greasy or shiny skin (seborrhoea) reflects high sebum output, which is both a driver and an outward sign of active acne, often concentrated in the T-zone.
Distribution along the central face, jawline, neck, upper chest, shoulders, and upper back marks out the regions richest in sebaceous follicles.
Emotional and psychosocial distress is measurable on validated quality-of-life scales (Cardiff Acne Disability Index, Acne-QoL), with severity often disproportionate to the visible burden.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent oiliness across the forehead, nose, and chin in a child aged 7-10 may signal premature adrenarche and predict moderate-to-severe acne in adolescence
First crops of closed comedones along the lateral cheeks and forehead in early puberty
A new ring of inflammatory papules along the jawline and chin in an adult woman, especially with premenstrual flares
Worsening lesions after starting anabolic steroids, high-dose corticosteroids, lithium, isoniazid, or certain progestin-only contraceptives
Acne accompanied by hirsutism, irregular menstrual cycles, scalp hair thinning, or rapid weight gain, which warrants workup for PCOS or other androgen excess
emergencySigns: Sudden ulcerative crusted acne with fever, joint pain, and malaise within 4-8 weeks of starting isotretinoin, suggesting acne fulminans, which requires halting isotretinoin and adding systemic corticosteroids
Severe rash, mouth or eye sores, peeling skin, or facial swelling on minocycline or doxycycline, which may indicate DRESS or Stevens-Johnson syndrome and requires stopping the drug and emergency care

Sudden onset of severe headache, vision change, or pulsatile tinnitus while on isotretinoin or tetracycline antibiotics, which may reflect idiopathic intracranial hypertension and requires urgent neurology review
Right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, or dark urine on oral isotretinoin or oral antibiotics, which suggests possible drug-induced hepatitis warranting same-day liver function tests
Suspected pregnancy on isotretinoin, which requires immediate contact with the prescribing clinician because the drug is a category X teratogen with a high risk of severe congenital malformations

causes.description: Testosterone is converted to the more potent dihydrotestosterone by 5-alpha-reductase inside the sebaceous gland. DHT stimulates sebocyte proliferation and lipid output. Sebum quantity correlates with disease severity, and patients with complete androgen insensitivity do not develop acne.

Keratinocytes lining the infundibulum fail to shed normally, clumping with corneocytes and sebum to form the microcomedo, the universal precursor of every acne lesion. Retinoids correct this step, which is why they form the backbone of nearly all treatment regimens.

causes.cause: Cutibacterium acnes biofilm and inflammatory phylotypes
causes.description: Cutibacterium acnes is part of the normal skin microbiome, but specific virulent phylotypes (notably IA1) form biofilms inside the obstructed follicle, secrete lipases and porphyrins, and activate innate immunity through Toll-like receptor 2 and the NLRP3 inflammasome (Dreno JEADV 2018).

causes.cause: Innate and adaptive inflammation (IL-1 beta, IL-17, TNF)
causes.description: Inflammation begins before the visible lesion appears, with peri-follicular IL-1 beta and CD4 T-cell infiltrates detectable around clinically normal-looking skin in acne patients. IL-17-mediated Th17 responses drive the chronicity that distinguishes acne from a transient follicular infection.

causes.cause: Insulin and IGF-1 signalling and dietary factors
causes.description: Hyperinsulinaemia and elevated insulin-like growth factor 1 stimulate the mammalian target of rapamycin complex 1 (mTORC1), increasing sebocyte lipogenesis and follicular keratinocyte proliferation. High-glycaemic-load diets and skim-milk intake have been linked in cohort studies to acne severity, though effect sizes are modest.

Polycystic ovary syndrome, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, Cushing syndrome, and androgen-secreting tumours can all present with acne. Anabolic steroids, high-dose corticosteroids, danazol, and certain progestin-only contraceptives also precipitate or worsen disease.

Lithium, isoniazid, phenytoin, EGFR inhibitors (acneiform eruption), high-dose B vitamins, and certain Chinese herbal supplements containing exogenous corticosteroids can mimic or exacerbate acne. Pomades, comedogenic cosmetics, and chronic occlusion from helmets, masks, or sports gear cause acne mechanica.

Twin studies estimate heritability for acne at around 80%. Family history in a first-degree relative roughly doubles personal risk and predicts more severe and persistent disease. Specific susceptibility loci identified in genome-wide association studies map to inflammatory and androgen-pathway genes.

riskFactors.description: Puberty triggers a 10-fold rise in adrenal and gonadal androgens; nearly 85% of adolescents are affected to some degree, and severity peaks at ages 14-19.
Heritability is approximately 80% in twin studies. A parent or sibling with severe or scarring acne roughly doubles personal risk and predicts more severe and persistent disease.
Adult-onset and persistent acne affect women three to four times more often than men, with jawline distribution and premenstrual flares characteristic.

Up to 50% of women with PCOS have acne; screening for PCOS is warranted in women with acne plus hirsutism, irregular cycles, or scalp hair thinning, because treating the underlying disorder improves the skin.

Randomized trials of low-glycaemic-load diets show modest improvements in lesion counts. Observational data link skim-milk and whey-protein intake to higher acne prevalence, likely via insulin and IGF-1 signalling.

riskFactors.factor: Anabolic steroids, corticosteroids, and certain medications
riskFactors.description: Anabolic steroids, high-dose corticosteroids, lithium, isoniazid, EGFR inhibitors, danazol, and progestin-only contraceptives with androgenic activity all worsen acne; identifying and substituting the trigger drug often clears the rash.
Helmets, chin straps, surgical and N95 masks (maskne), tight athletic wear, and habitual face-touching produce occlusive acne mechanica on the affected sites.

Heavy oil-based hair and skin products applied along the hairline and cheeks predictably produce acne cosmetica and acne pomade; switching to non-comedogenic formulations clears the eruption within weeks.

riskFactors.factor: Smoking and elevated body mass index
riskFactors.description: Cohort data link smoking and obesity to a higher prevalence and severity of adult acne, particularly the inflammatory comedonal subtype in women.
Acute stress and short sleep duration are associated with flare-ups in adolescents and adults, mediated through cortisol-driven sebum output and neuropeptide release in the skin.

Acne is equally common across ethnicities but post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and keloidal scarring are more frequent and prolonged in Fitzpatrick skin types IV-VI, warranting earlier and more aggressive treatment.

affectedDemographics: Approximately 50 million people in the United States have acne in any given year (AAD patient data; Reynolds JAAD 2024)
Adolescent peak prevalence is 85% in those aged 12-24 (Bhate & Williams BJD 2013)
Persistent adult acne affects roughly 12% of women and 3% of men aged 25 and older
Skin of colour patients carry equal incidence but disproportionate burden of post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and scarring
Acne is the 8th most prevalent disease globally and the leading skin disease by years lived with disability (Global Burden of Disease)
diagnosisOverview: Acne is a clinical diagnosis, with no blood or imaging test required to confirm it. A dermatologist or primary-care clinician examines the skin under good light and grades disease in three dimensions: lesion type (comedonal, papulopustular, nodulocystic), severity (mild, moderate, severe, using the Investigator's Global Assessment or Comprehensive Acne Severity Scale), and distribution (face, chest, back). Lesion counts of comedones, inflammatory papules and pustules, and nodules are recorded at baseline and on follow-up. Photographs taken under standardized lighting are useful for tracking response, since visible improvement often lags clinical change. Workup is reserved for atypical presentations. Hormonal evaluation, with serum total and free testosterone, DHEAS, 17-hydroxyprogesterone, prolactin, and LH/FSH, is indicated in women with sudden-onset or treatment-resistant acne accompanied by hirsutism, irregular menses, scalp hair loss, or rapid virilization, especially when polycystic ovary syndrome, late-onset congenital adrenal hyperplasia, or an androgen-secreting tumour is suspected. Pelvic ultrasound is added when PCOS is on the differential. In children under 7, an acne eruption is itself an indication for endocrine evaluation because adrenarche should not have occurred yet. Bacterial swabs are reserved for treatment failure or unusual presentations, mainly to identify gram-negative folliculitis after prolonged antibiotic therapy. Skin biopsy is rarely needed but is performed when the diagnosis is uncertain or when conditions such as acne rosacea, sarcoidosis, lupus miliaris disseminatus faciei, or cutaneous tuberculosis are being considered. The decisive differentials are rosacea (centrofacial flushing and telangiectasia without comedones), folliculitis (monomorphic pustules at follicular orifices, often after antibiotic use or hot-tub exposure), perioral dermatitis (small papules sparing the vermilion border), and hidradenitis suppurativa (recurrent painful nodules and sinuses in flexural areas).

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination with lesion count and severity grading
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the diagnosis based on lesion morphology and distribution, grades severity, and selects the appropriate therapy ladder. The Investigator's Global Assessment and Comprehensive Acne Severity Scale are the most widely used scales in clinics and trials.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician examines the face, chest, back, and shoulders under good light, counts comedones, papules, pustules, and nodules, and records distribution. The visit takes 10-20 minutes; no blood or imaging is needed for typical disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Tracks response objectively over weeks to months, since patients often perceive less improvement than the photographs reveal. Particularly useful in adult women on hormonal therapy and patients on isotretinoin.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Photographs of the front face and each side are taken at every visit in consistent lighting and angle. Patients remove makeup and pull hair back; no preparation is needed beyond a clean face.

diagnosticTests.test: Hormonal panel (total/free testosterone, DHEAS, 17-OH-progesterone, prolactin, LH/FSH)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Indicated in women with acne plus hirsutism, irregular cycles, scalp hair thinning, or rapid virilization, and in any pre-pubertal child with acne, to identify PCOS, late-onset congenital adrenal hyperplasia, or androgen-secreting tumour.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard morning blood draw, ideally in the early follicular phase (day 3-5 of the cycle) for women still menstruating. Results return in 2-5 days; the prescribing clinician interprets them in context.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Added when PCOS is suspected, to assess ovarian morphology (Rotterdam criteria require two of three: oligo-ovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries on imaging).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute transabdominal and, in sexually active women, transvaginal ultrasound. No sedation or radiation; mild discomfort with the transvaginal probe.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for treatment failure or atypical pustular eruptions to identify gram-negative folliculitis (Klebsiella, Enterobacter, Proteus) that can complicate long-term antibiotic therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile swab is rolled over the affected lesion for a few seconds; results return in 24-72 hours. Mild stinging is the only discomfort.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Before starting oral isotretinoin, AAD 2024 recommends baseline complete blood count, fasting lipid panel, and liver function tests, with repeat lipids and LFTs at 2-month intervals while on therapy. Routine creatine kinase and inflammatory markers are not required.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single fasting blood draw before the first prescription and serial follow-up draws aligned with monthly iPLEDGE visits in the United States.

diagnosticTests.test: Pregnancy testing (women of reproductive potential on isotretinoin)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Mandatory monthly serum or urine pregnancy testing throughout isotretinoin therapy plus 1 month after, with two reliable contraceptive methods, under the US iPLEDGE risk-management programme.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Each month a pregnancy test is performed in clinic or laboratory and the result entered into the iPLEDGE portal before the pharmacy will dispense the next 30-day supply.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Performed only when the diagnosis is uncertain, when treatment-resistant lesions look atypical, or when conditions such as sarcoidosis, lupus miliaris disseminatus faciei, or cutaneous tuberculosis are being considered.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 3-4 mm punch biopsy under local anaesthetic with 1-2 sutures, taking 10-15 minutes in clinic. Histology returns in 1-2 weeks.

treatmentOverview: Acne treatment is structured around severity, lesion type, and patient factors, codified by the American Academy of Dermatology 2024 Guidelines of Care (Reynolds JAAD 2024) and the European EuroGuiDerm 2024 recommendations. The shared principle is to address as many pathogenic steps as possible while keeping the regimen tolerable enough to use for the 12-24 weeks needed for meaningful response. Mild comedonal disease starts with a topical retinoid (adapalene 0.1 or 0.3% gel, tretinoin 0.025-0.1%, or tazarotene 0.045-0.1%) plus benzoyl peroxide 2.5-5% to address hyperkeratinization and Cutibacterium acnes simultaneously. Moderate papulopustular disease layers in a topical antibiotic such as clindamycin 1%, usually as a fixed-dose combination with benzoyl peroxide and adapalene to limit antibiotic resistance, and the AAD 2024 fixed triple-combination clindamycin/benzoyl peroxide/adapalene gel is approved as first-line. The newer non-antibiotic topical clascoterone 1% cream blocks the androgen receptor in sebocytes and is licensed in patients aged 12 and older. Moderate-to-severe disease that fails topicals adds a systemic agent: oral doxycycline (40-100 mg daily) or sarecycline (a narrow-spectrum tetracycline at 1.5 mg/kg daily) for 3-4 months, always co-prescribed with a topical retinoid plus benzoyl peroxide to reduce relapse and resistance. In women, AAD 2024 endorses combined oral contraceptives containing oestrogen plus a low-androgenic progestin and spironolactone (50-200 mg daily) as effective alternatives or adjuncts to oral antibiotics, with the SAFA randomized trial (Santer BMJ 2023) showing significantly better Acne-Specific Quality-of-Life and lesion outcomes versus placebo at 24 weeks. Severe nodulocystic disease, scarring acne, treatment-resistant moderate disease, and acne with major psychosocial impact are indications for oral isotretinoin (typically 0.5-1.0 mg/kg/day to a cumulative dose of 120-150 mg/kg over 5-7 months), which is the only therapy that targets all four pathogenic steps and produces durable remission in 70-85% of patients. Isotretinoin is a category X teratogen managed under iPLEDGE in the United States and equivalent risk-management programmes elsewhere. Procedural adjuncts such as chemical peels, laser and light therapy, comedone extraction, intralesional corticosteroid for individual cysts, and scar-treatment lasers and microneedling once active disease is controlled round out the toolkit.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical retinoids (adapalene 0.1% or 0.3% gel; tretinoin 0.025-0.1% cream or microsphere gel; tazarotene 0.045% lotion or 0.1% cream; trifarotene 0.005% cream)
medicalTreatments.description: The cornerstone of nearly every acne regimen at every severity. Normalizes follicular keratinization, dissolves microcomedones, and has anti-inflammatory effects. Applied as a pea-sized amount to the whole affected area at night, with sunscreen by day to manage photosensitivity. Pregnancy contraindicated for tazarotene; adapalene is the preferred retinoid in pregnancy planning.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces total lesion counts by 40-70% at 12 weeks; combined with benzoyl peroxide outperforms either alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Benzoyl peroxide 2.5-10% (wash, gel, or cream, applied once or twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Bactericidal against Cutibacterium acnes without inducing resistance, and mildly comedolytic. Always combined with a topical or systemic antibiotic to limit resistance. Bleaches fabrics and hair; use white towels and avoid contact with coloured clothing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces inflammatory lesions by 40-60% at 12 weeks; lower concentrations (2.5-5%) are nearly as effective as 10% with less irritation.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical antibiotics (clindamycin 1% solution, gel, or foam; erythromycin 2%, less preferred due to resistance)
medicalTreatments.description: Used only in fixed combinations with benzoyl peroxide or a retinoid to reduce Cutibacterium acnes resistance, which now exceeds 50% globally. Never used as monotherapy under AAD 2024.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces inflammatory lesions by 50-65% at 12 weeks when combined with benzoyl peroxide or a retinoid.

medicalTreatments.name: Clindamycin 1.2% / benzoyl peroxide 3.1% / adapalene 0.15% fixed triple-combination gel (apply once daily)
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved 2023 fixed triple-combination, recommended in AAD 2024 as first-line topical for moderate inflammatory acne; targets all three accessible mechanisms simultaneously and improves adherence by collapsing three tubes into one.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Investigator's Global Assessment success (clear or almost-clear with at least 2-grade improvement) in roughly 50% at 12 weeks versus 25-30% for vehicle in pivotal trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Clascoterone 1% cream (apply twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First topical androgen-receptor antagonist licensed in the United States in 2020 for patients aged 12 and older. Blocks dihydrotestosterone signalling in the sebocyte and follicular keratinocyte. Safe in both sexes; transient mild local irritation is the main side effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Investigator's Global Assessment treatment success in roughly 18-20% at 12 weeks versus 7-8% with vehicle in pivotal trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral tetracyclines (doxycycline 40-100 mg once daily; sarecycline 1.5 mg/kg once daily; minocycline 50-100 mg once or twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral antibiotic for moderate-to-severe inflammatory acne. Always co-prescribed with a topical retinoid plus benzoyl peroxide and limited to 3-4 months to reduce Cutibacterium acnes resistance and dysbiosis. Sarecycline is narrow-spectrum and may have a lower gut-microbiome impact. Minocycline carries a higher risk of vestibular and pigmentary side effects than doxycycline.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces inflammatory lesion counts by 50-70% at 12 weeks when combined with topical therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Combined oral contraceptives (oestrogen plus a low-androgenic progestin such as drospirenone, norgestimate, or desogestrel)
medicalTreatments.description: Lower free testosterone via sex-hormone-binding globulin induction and block ovarian androgen output. AAD 2024 endorses combined oral contraceptives for women with moderate-to-severe acne, including jawline and premenstrual flares. Contraindicated in smokers over 35, uncontrolled hypertension, prior thromboembolism, and selected migraine subtypes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces inflammatory lesions by 30-55% at 6 months; non-inferior to oral antibiotics by 6 months and durable while continued.

medicalTreatments.name: Spironolactone (50-200 mg orally daily; commonly titrated from 50 to 100-150 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Aldosterone antagonist with anti-androgenic action at the sebocyte androgen receptor. Used in adult women, often as a maintenance alternative to oral antibiotics. Side effects (menstrual irregularity, breast tenderness, dizziness, mild hyperkalaemia in older patients) are usually manageable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: SAFA RCT (Santer BMJ 2023): Acne-QoL adjusted between-group difference of 1.27 favouring spironolactone at week 24; significantly higher proportion clear or almost-clear versus placebo.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral isotretinoin (0.5-1.0 mg/kg/day, cumulative dose 120-150 mg/kg over 5-7 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces sebum output by 80-90%, normalizes follicular keratinization, suppresses Cutibacterium acnes, and damps inflammation, the only drug to address all four pathogenic steps. Indicated in severe nodulocystic acne, scarring acne, treatment-resistant moderate disease, and disease with major psychosocial impact. Category X teratogen managed under iPLEDGE in the United States with monthly visits, pregnancy testing, and two reliable contraceptive methods; baseline and serial lipids and liver function tests. Mucocutaneous dryness is universal; depression risk is debated, with no causal link supported in recent meta-analyses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete clearance in 70-85% of patients by end of course (Vallerand BJD 2018); durable remission in roughly 70% at 2 years, with about 25-30% requiring a second course.

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional triamcinolone acetonide (2-5 mg/mL, 0.05-0.1 mL per nodule)
medicalTreatments.description: Single-lesion treatment for individual painful nodules and cysts. Provides rapid pain relief and flattening within 48-72 hours. Risk of focal atrophy and hypopigmentation if higher concentrations or excessive volumes are used.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Visible flattening in 80-90% of injected lesions within 48-72 hours; not a standalone therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical azelaic acid 15-20% (apply twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory, mildly comedolytic, and depigmenting, useful in skin of colour and pregnancy, where retinoids are contraindicated. Slower onset than retinoids but well tolerated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces inflammatory lesions by 35-45% at 12 weeks; particularly helpful for post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation.

medicalTreatments.name: Chemical peels and procedural adjuncts (salicylic acid 20-30% peels; superficial glycolic acid; comedone extraction; intense pulsed light)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive in-office procedures that accelerate comedonal clearance and help with post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation. Typically delivered as a series of 4-6 monthly treatments alongside topical therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces comedone counts by 30-50% across pooled trials; not a substitute for guideline-directed pharmacotherapy.

surgicalOptions.name: Excision and drainage of large acne cysts and sinus tracts
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for fluctuant, painful, recurrent cysts and acne conglobata sinuses that fail medical therapy. Performed under local anaesthetic in office or minor-procedure suite.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic relief in 85-90% of treated lesions; recurrence is common without concurrent systemic therapy.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical and laser scar revision (subcision, punch excision, fractional CO2 or Er:YAG laser, microneedling with or without radiofrequency, dermabrasion)
surgicalOptions.description: Used once active acne is fully controlled to improve atrophic, boxcar, ice-pick, and rolling scars. Delivered as a series of treatments tailored to skin type and scar morphology.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 30-70% subjective improvement per session across modalities; multiple sessions and combined techniques produce the best outcomes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-glycaemic-load dietary pattern
alternativeTreatments.description: Randomized controlled trials of low-glycaemic-load diets show 20-30% reductions in total lesion counts and lower IGF-1 levels at 12 weeks. Reasonable as an adjunct, not a substitute for guideline therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tea tree oil (Melaleuca alternifolia) 5% gel
alternativeTreatments.description: A small randomized trial showed 5% tea tree oil reduced inflammatory lesions, slower than benzoyl peroxide 5% but with fewer side effects. Useful as a tolerability-driven adjunct in mild disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Niacinamide 4% gel or cream
alternativeTreatments.description: Topical niacinamide reduces inflammatory lesions in small randomized trials, comparable to clindamycin 1% gel without resistance risk. Well tolerated in pregnancy.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to dermatology when moderate or severe inflammatory or nodulocystic acne is present, when scarring has already appeared, when first-line topical therapy has failed at 12 weeks, when isotretinoin is being considered, when hormonal evaluation suggests an underlying endocrinopathy, when post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation is disproportionate to active lesions in skin of colour, and when psychosocial impact is significant regardless of objective severity. Adolescent boys with rapid-onset severe inflammatory acne and adult women with sudden-onset persistent acne benefit from early dermatology review to prevent permanent scarring.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified dermatologist with explicit experience prescribing isotretinoin under iPLEDGE (in the United States) or equivalent risk-management programmes, comfort with hormonal therapy in adult women (spironolactone, combined oral contraceptives), and access to in-office procedures such as intralesional steroid, comedone extraction, chemical peels, and scar revision lasers. Skin-of-colour expertise matters: ask whether the clinic stocks lower-strength retinoids and azelaic acid and whether they treat post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation actively. Continuity is high-yield, since acne is a 6-24 month treatment relationship at minimum.
preventionStrategies: Start a topical retinoid plus benzoyl peroxide at the first sign of comedones in early adolescence, since earlier treatment prevents progression to inflammatory and scarring disease
Switch comedogenic cosmetics, pomades, and hair products to non-comedogenic, oil-free formulations and avoid heavy occlusive sunscreens on acne-prone areas
Reduce dietary glycaemic load and skim-milk intake where appetite and culture allow, a modest evidence base but reasonable for patients motivated to try

Review prescription and over-the-counter medications for known acne-inducing agents (lithium, isoniazid, anabolic steroids, high-dose corticosteroids, certain progestin-only contraceptives) and substitute where clinically appropriate
Limit unnecessary face-touching and skin-picking and resist DIY extraction of inflammatory lesions, which deepens lesions and worsens scarring
Wash twice daily with a gentle non-soap cleanser and lukewarm water, since over-washing and scrubbing strip the barrier and worsen acne
Use a broad-spectrum non-comedogenic sunscreen (SPF 30+) daily, especially while on retinoids and tetracyclines, both of which raise photosensitivity
lifestyleModifications: Apply the prescribed regimen consistently, since at least 8-12 weeks of daily use are needed before judging response and stopping early is the leading reason for treatment failure
Build the topical regimen up slowly: start retinoids every other night for 2 weeks before going nightly, and pair with a fragrance-free moisturizer to manage retinoid dermatitis
Use a fragrance-free, ceramide-rich moisturizer in the morning and evening, especially while on retinoids, benzoyl peroxide, and isotretinoin, which all dry the skin
Wear broad-spectrum sunscreen daily; retinoids and tetracyclines both increase photosensitivity, and sun exposure worsens post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation in skin of colour
Shower and change out of sweaty clothing promptly after exercise to limit acne mechanica on the chest, shoulders, and back
Manage stress and protect sleep, since both reduce cortisol-driven sebum output and break the stress-flare cycle documented in adolescents and adults
Track flares in a simple diary with diet, products, menstrual cycle, and medication entries; personal triggers often emerge within a few months
Discuss vaccination plans, blood donation, and elective surgery timing with the prescribing clinician before starting isotretinoin

recommended: Low-glycaemic-load eating pattern with wholegrains, legumes, vegetables, and lean protein, which lowers insulin and IGF-1, with modest improvement in lesion counts
Mediterranean-style diet rich in olive oil, vegetables, fish, and nuts, associated with lower acne severity in cohort studies
Oily fish and other sources of omega-3 fatty acids, with small randomized trials showing modest reductions in inflammatory lesions
Green tea and other polyphenol-rich beverages, anti-inflammatory and weakly anti-androgenic in small trials
Adequate water intake to support healthy skin barrier function
avoid: High-glycaemic-index foods consumed in large quantities, such as white bread, sugary drinks, and refined snacks, linked to higher acne severity in randomized trials
Skim milk and whey-protein supplements in motivated patients with persistent acne, given an observational link to higher prevalence
Excessive intake of B12 and B6 supplements, which can trigger acneiform eruptions in susceptible patients
Crash diets and ketogenic diets that paradoxically raise IGF-1 and worsen acne in some patients
Anabolic steroids, SARMs, and unregulated bodybuilding supplements, where frequent and severe acne is a hallmark side effect

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is encouraged for its mood, sleep, and metabolic benefits and does not need to be modified for acne. Shower or rinse promptly after sweating, change out of damp sportswear, and avoid leaning helmets, pads, and straps directly on the face for prolonged periods to limit acne mechanica. During isotretinoin therapy, avoid heavy weight-lifting in the first few weeks, when transient muscle aches and creatine-kinase elevations are most common, and stop strenuous exercise if you develop joint pain or unusual muscle weakness.
dailyManagement: Cleanse twice daily with a gentle non-soap cleanser, pat dry, then apply prescribed topicals to the whole affected area, not just to visible lesions
Apply a fragrance-free non-comedogenic moisturizer in the morning and evening to manage retinoid- and benzoyl-peroxide-induced dryness
Use a broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen every morning, especially on retinoids, tetracyclines, and isotretinoin
Take oral medications at consistent times: tetracyclines with a full glass of water and avoiding lying down for 30 minutes, isotretinoin with a fatty meal to maximize absorption
Track flares, side effects, and missed doses in a brief diary and bring it to follow-up visits to refine the regimen
Keep a short-course intralesional steroid or oral antibiotic plan agreed with the clinician for early treatment of new painful nodules
prognosis: With a guideline-directed regimen, the outlook is excellent for the large majority of patients. Mild and moderate acne typically achieves at least 50% reduction in lesion counts by 12 weeks of consistent topical therapy, and clear or almost-clear skin in 60-75% by 6 months. Combined oral contraceptives and spironolactone produce comparable rates in adult women, with the SAFA trial confirming durable response on spironolactone at 24 weeks. A standard 5-7 month course of oral isotretinoin clears 70-85% of severe nodulocystic patients, with durable remission in roughly 70% at 2 years; about 25-30% need a second course. Scarring is the main long-term complication and is largely preventable with early effective treatment. Most adolescents see active lesions decline through their early twenties, although roughly 12% of women and 3% of men carry persistent or recurrent disease into adulthood, and a smaller proportion experience late-onset disease. Acne carries a measurable psychosocial burden, with evidence linking moderate-to-severe acne to clinically significant depression, anxiety, and suicidal ideation independent of objective lesion counts, and effective skin clearance reliably improves these measures.
recoveryTimeline: Topical retinoids and benzoyl peroxide produce visible improvement at 4-6 weeks and meaningful reduction in lesion counts by 12 weeks. Oral antibiotic plus topical regimens reduce inflammatory lesions by half in 8-12 weeks; antibiotics are limited to 3-4 months total to reduce resistance. Combined oral contraceptives and spironolactone take 3-6 months to reach peak effect, often with a transient worsening in the first 4-8 weeks. Isotretinoin courses run 5-7 months at 0.5-1.0 mg/kg/day to a cumulative dose of 120-150 mg/kg, with response continuing for up to 4 months after the last dose. Post-inflammatory erythema fades over 3-6 months and hyperpigmentation in skin of colour can take 6-18 months. Atrophic scars are permanent without procedural treatment, which is started only once active acne has been controlled for at least 6-12 months.
complications: Atrophic, hypertrophic, and keloidal scarring, preventable with early effective treatment but largely permanent once established
Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, flat brown or grey-brown marks lasting 6-18 months, more frequent and longer-lasting in Fitzpatrick skin types IV-VI
Post-inflammatory erythema, flat pink-red marks more visible in lighter skin types and typically fading over 3-6 months
Significant psychosocial impact, including depression, anxiety, social withdrawal, and increased risk of suicidal ideation independent of objective lesion counts

Acne fulminans, an abrupt onset of severe ulcerative acne with fever, joint pain, leukocytosis, and elevated inflammatory markers, sometimes triggered by starting isotretinoin without corticosteroid coverage
Secondary bacterial infection of large cysts and sinus tracts, occasionally requiring incision and drainage and broader-spectrum antibiotic therapy
Gram-negative folliculitis after prolonged oral antibiotic therapy, presenting as a sudden crop of monomorphic pustules around the central face

Antibiotic-associated complications including Clostridioides difficile colitis, vaginal candidiasis, photosensitivity (tetracyclines), and minocycline-induced pigmentation, autoimmune hepatitis, or lupus-like syndrome

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Acne
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that publishes the AAD acne guidelines, with practical advice on washing, moisturizers, and procedural options.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — Acne Patient Information
supportResources.description: Peer-reviewed UK patient leaflets covering acne severity, treatment options, and isotretinoin counselling.

supportResources.name: DermNet — Acne Vulgaris
supportResources.description: Detailed peer-reviewed clinical and patient-facing content from DermNet New Zealand, used by clinicians worldwide.

supportResources.name: Acne Support (UK)
supportResources.description: British Association of Dermatologists-endorsed acne resource hub with patient stories, treatment guides, and isotretinoin information.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rosacea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rosacea presents with centrofacial flushing, persistent erythema, telangiectasia, and papulopustules without comedones, typically in adults over 30. Comedones essentially exclude rosacea and confirm acne. Triggers (heat, alcohol, spicy food, sunlight) and treatment overlap is partial; brimonidine, ivermectin, and topical metronidazole are rosacea-specific.

confusedWithConditions.name: Perioral Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Perioral dermatitis produces small monomorphic erythematous papules and micropustules in the perinasal, perioral, and periocular skin, classically sparing a thin rim of skin around the vermilion border. There are no comedones. It is often triggered by topical corticosteroids; treatment requires steroid withdrawal plus oral tetracycline or topical metronidazole.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hidradenitis Suppurativa
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hidradenitis suppurativa produces recurrent painful nodules, abscesses, sinus tracts, and scarring in axillae, groin, inframammary folds, and buttocks rather than the face. Although a follicular disease, it requires distinct therapy (adalimumab, weight loss, surgery) and is much more disabling than typical acne.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pseudofolliculitis Barbae
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pseudofolliculitis barbae is an ingrown-hair eruption in the beard area, common in men with curly hair, with papules and pustules clustered around hair shafts that re-enter the skin. Comedones are absent. Treatment focuses on shaving technique, depilation, and eflornithine; standard acne therapy is rarely sufficient.

confusedWithConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Seborrheic dermatitis produces greasy yellow scale on the scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, and chest (sebum-rich areas) driven by Malassezia. It can coexist with acne but lacks comedones and pustule-tipped papules, and responds to ketoconazole, ciclopirox, and short-course topical corticosteroids.

relatedConditions.name: Rosacea
relatedConditions.relevance: Leading differential and frequent co-existing facial inflammatory dermatosis in adults; managed by the same specialty with overlapping topical options.

relatedConditions.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Up to 50% of women with PCOS have acne; identifying and treating the underlying endocrinopathy improves skin outcomes.

relatedConditions.name: Hidradenitis Suppurativa
relatedConditions.relevance: Follicular occlusive disease in the same spectrum; co-occurs in patients with severe inflammatory acne and acne conglobata.

relatedConditions.name: Perioral Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Common acneiform mimic around the mouth and nose; corticosteroid history distinguishes it.

relatedConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-existing sebum-driven inflammatory dermatosis in adolescents and adults; shared lifestyle and topical care.

relatedConditions.name: Cushing's Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Endocrinopathy that can present with adult-onset acne plus weight gain, striae, and hypertension; warrants screening when clinical features fit.

faqs.question: Is acne curable?
faqs.answer: Acne is not curable in the strict sense because the underlying tendency to oily skin and follicular plugging persists, but it is highly controllable. Mild and moderate acne usually clears or near-clears within 3-6 months of consistent topical therapy. A 5-7 month course of oral isotretinoin produces durable remission in roughly 70% of severe patients at 2 years, with about a quarter needing a second course. Most adolescents see active disease decline through their early twenties.

faqs.question: What causes acne?
faqs.answer: Acne is caused by four interacting processes inside the hair follicle: excess sebum production driven by androgens, abnormal shedding of keratinocytes that plugs the follicle, overgrowth of Cutibacterium acnes biofilms, and inflammation through Toll-like receptor 2 and the NLRP3 inflammasome. Genetics set susceptibility (heritability around 80%), and hormones, certain medications, diet, and mechanical occlusion modify expression.

faqs.question: What does the early stage of acne look like?
faqs.answer: Early acne presents as small skin-coloured or whitish bumps (closed comedones) and dark-tipped pores (open comedones, or blackheads) across the forehead, nose, and chin in early adolescence. Visible inflammation, red papules and pus-tipped pustules, usually follows within months. Early treatment with a topical retinoid plus benzoyl peroxide at this stage prevents progression to inflammatory and scarring disease.

faqs.question: How is acne diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Acne is diagnosed clinically. A dermatologist or primary-care clinician examines the face, chest, and back, counts lesion types, and grades severity using scales such as the Investigator's Global Assessment. Blood tests are reserved for women with sudden-onset or treatment-resistant acne plus hirsutism, irregular cycles, or hair thinning, where polycystic ovary syndrome or other androgen excess is suspected, and for children with acne before age 7.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for acne?
faqs.answer: There is no single best treatment; the right regimen depends on severity and lesion type. Mild comedonal acne responds to a topical retinoid plus benzoyl peroxide. Moderate inflammatory disease usually adds a topical antibiotic in a fixed combination and, when needed, an oral tetracycline for 3-4 months. Adult women often benefit from spironolactone or a combined oral contraceptive. Severe nodulocystic or scarring acne is treated with oral isotretinoin under close monitoring.

faqs.question: How long does acne treatment take to work?
faqs.answer: Most topical and oral regimens take 8-12 weeks of consistent daily use before meaningful improvement, and full benefit at 4-6 months. The first 2-4 weeks can actually look worse as deeper lesions surface, and pushing through this period is essential. Isotretinoin courses run 5-7 months, with continued improvement for up to 4 months after the last dose.

faqs.question: Are topical retinoids safe long-term?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adapalene, tretinoin, tazarotene, and trifarotene are safe for years of nightly use when paired with sunscreen and moisturizer. Mild redness and peeling are common in the first 4-6 weeks and ease with a slower titration. Tazarotene is contraindicated in pregnancy and pregnancy planning; adapalene is the preferred retinoid for women trying to conceive.

faqs.question: What is isotretinoin and who needs it?
faqs.answer: Isotretinoin is an oral retinoid (Accutane, Roaccutane, Absorica, Claravis) that reduces sebum output by 80-90% and addresses all four pathogenic steps of acne. It is indicated for severe nodulocystic acne, scarring acne, treatment-resistant moderate acne, and acne with major psychosocial impact. Typical dosing is 0.5-1.0 mg/kg/day to a cumulative dose of 120-150 mg/kg over 5-7 months. It is a category X teratogen managed under iPLEDGE in the United States.

faqs.question: What is iPLEDGE?
faqs.answer: iPLEDGE is the US FDA risk-management programme for isotretinoin, designed to prevent in-utero exposure. Women of reproductive potential need monthly pregnancy tests, two reliable contraceptive methods, and monthly clinic visits to obtain each 30-day prescription. Men and women of non-reproductive potential have a lighter monitoring schedule but still require monthly visits.

faqs.question: Does isotretinoin cause depression?
faqs.answer: A causal link has not been established. Large meta-analyses, including population-level pharmaco-epidemiologic studies, have not shown a consistent increase in depression or suicide risk among patients on isotretinoin, and treatment of severe acne typically improves mood scores. Pre-existing mental-health conditions should be monitored throughout the course and any change in mood reported promptly.

faqs.question: Can adults get acne?
faqs.answer: Yes. Persistent or late-onset acne affects roughly 12% of women and 3% of men over age 25. Adult female acne is typically distributed along the jawline and chin with premenstrual flares. Hormonal therapy with combined oral contraceptives and spironolactone works particularly well in this group, with the SAFA trial confirming spironolactone's effectiveness at 24 weeks.

faqs.question: Does diet really affect acne?
faqs.answer: Diet has a modest but real effect in some patients. Low-glycaemic-load eating patterns reduce lesion counts by 20-30% in randomized trials, and observational data link skim-milk and whey-protein intake to higher acne prevalence. Chocolate, oily food, and individual culprits beyond glycaemic load have weaker evidence. Diet is an adjunct, not a substitute for guideline-directed therapy.

faqs.question: What is hormonal acne and how is it treated?
faqs.answer: Hormonal acne is the term used for adult female acne distributed along the jawline, chin, and lower face with premenstrual flares. Treatment options endorsed by the AAD 2024 guideline include combined oral contraceptives with a low-androgenic progestin and spironolactone 50-200 mg daily. Both take 3-6 months to reach peak effect and are often used alongside a topical retinoid.

faqs.question: Can I pop or squeeze my pimples?
faqs.answer: No. Squeezing inflammatory papules, pustules, and nodules deepens the lesion, increases the chance of scarring, and prolongs healing. Painful nodules and cysts can be flattened in 48-72 hours with an in-office intralesional steroid injection, which is far safer than self-extraction. Comedone extraction with a sterile loop is performed by clinicians where appropriate.

faqs.question: How do I prevent acne scars?
faqs.answer: The single best way to prevent scarring is early effective treatment of inflammatory and nodulocystic acne, escalating to systemic therapy when topical regimens fail rather than waiting for visible scars to appear. Avoid squeezing lesions, treat post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation actively in skin of colour, and use daily sunscreen to prevent darkening. Scar revision lasers and microneedling are used once active acne has been controlled for at least 6-12 months.

faqs.question: Does acne go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Most adolescent acne improves through the early twenties as androgen output stabilizes, but waiting it out risks permanent scarring and prolonged post-inflammatory pigmentation, especially in skin of colour. Roughly 12% of women and 3% of men carry persistent or recurrent acne into adulthood. Effective treatment shortens the active phase and prevents long-term complications.

faqs.question: Is acne caused by dirty skin?
faqs.answer: No. Acne is driven by hormones, follicular plugging, bacteria, and inflammation, not by surface dirt. Over-washing, scrubbing, and harsh cleansers strip the skin barrier and can worsen acne. Gentle non-soap cleansing twice daily plus a fragrance-free moisturizer is the right cleansing routine for nearly every patient.

faqs.question: Can stress cause acne?
faqs.answer: Stress does not cause acne but is a recognized trigger for flares. Cortisol-driven sebum output, sleep disruption, and skin-picking during stressful periods all contribute. Adolescents commonly flare around exam periods. Sleep hygiene, regular exercise, and where indicated cognitive-behavioural support reduce flare frequency in chronic disease.

faqs.question: How much does acne treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Generic topical retinoids, benzoyl peroxide, and clindamycin together cost roughly USD 30-80 per month in the US and similar in the UK; in India and other emerging markets, generic topical regimens are widely affordable. Oral antibiotics and isotretinoin generics cost USD 20-150 per month. Brand-name and newer agents (clascoterone, trifarotene, sarecycline, the fixed triple-combination gel) run several hundred dollars per month at US list price, with insurance coverage variable.

faqs.question: Can pregnant women treat acne safely?
faqs.answer: Yes, with care. Safe options in pregnancy include topical azelaic acid, topical erythromycin or clindamycin (combined with benzoyl peroxide), and oral erythromycin for severe inflammatory disease. Topical retinoids and tazarotene, oral tetracyclines, isotretinoin, hormonal therapy, and spironolactone are contraindicated. Discuss any regimen with the prescribing clinician early in pregnancy or pregnancy planning.

faqs.question: When should I see a dermatologist for acne?
faqs.answer: See a dermatologist for moderate-to-severe inflammatory or nodulocystic acne, any scarring or significant pigmentation, treatment failure after 12 weeks of consistent first-line therapy, suspected hormonal disease, and acne with significant psychosocial impact. Adolescent boys with rapidly progressive severe disease and adult women with sudden-onset persistent acne especially benefit from early specialist review to prevent permanent scarring.

metaDescription: Acne vulgaris: pimples, blackheads, and cysts on face, chest, and back. Symptoms, causes, treatment, and prognosis reviewed by our medical board.
